reasoning: Applied error taxonomy to 20 errors, selecting 7 with exercise_priority >= 3 for question generation. Targeted the three dominant patterns: null subject omission (5 instances, priority 5), false friends (4 instances, priority 5), and prepositional calques (4 instances, priority 4). Used ZPD calibration from level assessment (B2 lower bound, C1 upper bound) to distribute difficulty: 2 zpd_lower (confidence building with recognition tasks), 3 zpd_target (at the B2/C1 boundary), 2 zpd_stretch (production tasks pushing toward C1). Distractors incorporate the student's actual errors and Spanish L1 transfer patterns per interlanguage theory. Question types mapped per error-to-question table: gap (blank) for production of correct forms, gap (choices) for preposition selection, choice for false friend recognition, boolean for rule checking, categorizer for collocation patterns.

source_ref.original: many people think is not enough flexibility
source_ref.corrected: many people think it is not enough flexibility

payload.title: Complete with the missing subject
payload.template_text: Many of my colleagues think {0} is not enough flexibility in our hybrid model.
payload.explanation: In English, embedded clauses need an explicit subject. 'I think IT is' - the expletive 'it' cannot be dropped, unlike in Spanish where the subject is implied by the verb conjugation.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'many people think is not enough flexibility'

source_ref.original: I think is a mix of both
source_ref.corrected: I think it is a mix of both

payload.title: Fill in the missing word
payload.template_text: The problem is that {0} is very difficult to define what counts as automation.
payload.explanation: English requires an explicit subject in every clause. 'The problem is that IT is...' - the expletive 'it' is mandatory even when the real subject comes later.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'the problem is that is very difficult to define'

source_ref.original: I had to assist to many meetings
source_ref.corrected: I had to attend many meetings

payload.title: Which sentence uses the correct word?

choices.text: I had to assist to many unproductive meetings last week.
I had to attend many unproductive meetings last week.
I had to assist many unproductive meetings last week.

payload.explanation: Spanish 'asistir a' = English 'attend'. English 'assist' means 'help'. 'Attend a meeting' is the correct collocation.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'I had to assist to many meetings'

source_ref.original: the results were very sensible to how you measure them
source_ref.corrected: the results were very sensitive to how you measure them

payload.title: Choose the correct word
payload.template_text: The productivity data is very {0} to the measurement methodology you choose.
payload.explanation: Spanish 'sensible' = English 'sensitive' (responsive to stimuli or changes). English 'sensible' = Spanish 'sensato' (reasonable, practical).
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'the results were very sensible to how you measure them'

payload.title: Sort these phrases by the correct preposition: ON or OF
payload.explanation: Spanish 'de' maps to different English prepositions depending on the word: depend ON, rely ON, dependent ON, but consist OF, independent OF, aware OF. These must be memorized as fixed collocations.

source_ref.original: I am agree with the hybrid model
source_ref.corrected: I agree with the hybrid model

payload.title: 'I am agree with the hybrid model' is a correct English sentence.
payload.explanation: In English, 'agree' is a verb, not an adjective. Say 'I agree with' (not 'I am agree with'). The Spanish structure 'estoy de acuerdo' uses the verb 'to be' + adjective, but in English 'agree' functions as the main verb directly.

source_ref.original: We need to make research on this
source_ref.corrected: We need to do research on this

payload.title: Complete with the correct verb (make, do, give, hold)
payload.template_text: Last week I had to {0} a presentation to the partners, {1} a meeting with the regional directors, and {2} research on the client's competitors.
payload.explanation: English business collocations: GIVE a presentation (not 'do'), HOLD a meeting (or 'have'; not 'make'), DO research (or 'conduct'; not 'make'). These don't follow logical rules - they must be memorized as fixed pairs.
payload.content: In the lesson you said: 'make research', 'make a meeting', 'do a presentation'
